Q: Used targeted practice after learning each concept rather than random Quant sets.
Reviewed not only wrong Quant answers but also correct answers that took too long.
Treated Quant timing as a decision problem: know when to invest time and when to move on.
V: For CR, stopped jumping straight to answers and first identified the main conclusion and evidence.
After reading the CR question stem, paused to clarify what the task required before evaluating choices.
Evaluated each answer choice independently rather than hunting for a preselected answer.
Used focused CR sets to actively engage with argument structure.
For RC, spent four to six minutes on the first read and used micro-breaks to process chunks.
For difficult RC, mentally paraphrased every few sentences.
Focused on passage structure, main point, paragraph relationships, evidence, and opinion rather than every technical detail.
DI: Used RC skills to handle DI information load without panicking.
Scanned DI prompts to understand structure first, then found only the information needed for each question.
Treated DI like using an index rather than reading every tab cover to cover.
Accepted leaving two or three questions if that protected accuracy on the rest.
Focused on completion with decent accuracy rather than perfection.
overall: Improved from 445 to 675 over about five months while working full-time.
Started with official-book self-study but moved to e-GMAT for more structure.
Studied about 1.5 to 2 hours on weekdays and more than five hours on weekends when possible.
Chose study times based on when focus was strongest rather than counting raw hours.
Used Verbal, Quant, DI section order: Verbal fresh, Quant in the middle, DI last.
Valued mocks for stamina more than score prediction.
Made error analysis after every practice set non-negotiable.

Q: Used the first diagnostic to estimate the amount and section focus of prep needed.
Started with free resources, GMAT Club question bank, and the Official Guide.
Moved to TTP Quant after realizing unstructured Quant study was not improving scores.
Completed almost all TTP Quant topics in the second month.
Focused first on understanding each topic, then practicing easy to medium to hard questions.
Revisited past Quant topics regularly to keep concepts fresh.
On test day, ignored a wrong first Quant question and focused only on the question in front.
V: Maintained Verbal through official questions and official mocks once it was already high.
Used GMAT Ninja videos for RC and CR concepts.
Practiced eliminating wrong answers by explaining why each option was wrong.
For CR, asked why the chosen conclusion was actually the conclusion, helping identify premises.
For RC, summarized paragraphs on the first read so later questions could be answered quickly.
DI: Started DI later after Quant and Verbal foundations, following common expert advice.
Practiced DI with official questions only.
Bought 100 extra official questions for Quant and DI.
Took the break after Verbal to be fresh for DI.
Found official and real DI broadly similar, though time management remained a challenge.
overall: Improved from a 535 diagnostic to 715 on the first official attempt after about 450 hours of study.
Studied intensely for roughly two and a half months before the semester began.
Divided the week by section, studying only one major section on a given day.
Used official mocks only, taking ten mock attempts and simulating test-center conditions in a library.
Booked the exam once scoring consistently around 655, leaving two weeks to sharpen weaknesses.
Did many mocks to reduce test anxiety and make the official exam feel like another mock.
Accepted score drops as part of the process rather than second-guessing the whole plan.
Emphasized a sustainable rhythm to avoid fatigue late in prep.

Q: Built Quant concepts with the Ultimate GMAT Quantitative Preparation Guide and GMAT Club Math Book.
Used GMAT Club forum tags to find credible Quant questions, especially GMAT Prep-tagged questions.
Used GMAT Club tests mainly for Quant because they tested concepts deeply.
Used TTP trial only briefly, noting some TTP questions were high quality.
In the two-week retake push, used mock reports and awareness to pinpoint Quant weak areas.
Practiced official mock questions tagged GMAT Prep and GMAT Prep Focus at 655+ difficulty.
On the successful attempt, took Quant first, finished with over 10 minutes left, and reviewed bookmarked questions.
V: Used PowerScore CR Bible to build concepts.
Used GMAT Ninja YouTube videos to learn common Verbal mistakes.
Used official questions almost exclusively for Verbal practice.
Used GMAT Prep and GMAT Prep Focus tags on GMAT Club for official-style Verbal practice.
During the retake push, analyzed RC and CR misses from the prior official attempt and filled gaps with more official questions.
DI: Used OG and DI Review online package for DI practice.
Found DI Review questions somewhat harder than the real exam but still useful.
Used GMAT Ninja DI videos for strong question selection and explanations.
Used GMAT Club only for old official-mock DI questions with the GMAT Prep tag.
Recognized DI as the weakest section and practiced most DI weak areas in the two-week retake push.
Learned from spending too much time on an MSR, then guessing much of it and rushing later questions.
overall: Improved from 675 to 735 in two weeks after a first official attempt exposed timing and weak-area issues.
Prepared inconsistently at first, then concentrated more effort during vacation.
Used only official mocks and treated official Verbal as closely representative of the real exam.
Changed final section order back to Quant, Verbal, DI because Quant was the backbone and confidence builder.
Used the two retake weeks only to drill pinpointed weak areas, not to restart broadly.
Did not blindly solve questions; analyzed mistakes and filled gaps with targeted official practice.
Saw DI as generally tougher than mocks and Quant/Verbal as similar to official mocks.
